queries.method_core: Linear dispersion-relation stability analysis of a two-component, locally-nonlinear dynamical system coupled diffusively across a network, in which the network's Laplacian operator replaces the continuous diffusion operator and its eigenmodes serve as generalized Fourier modes. A uniform fixed point that is stable to homogeneous perturbations is destabilized along particular Laplacian eigenmodes when the two components have sufficiently different transport (coupling) rates; nonlinear saturation then splits the units into two self-reinforcing groups, with only units whose degree lies near a critical value participating, and with multistability and hysteresis captured by a degree-class mean-field closure.
queries.query_object: coupled self-amplifying / suppressing kinetics on the nodes of a large random network

hypotheticals.generic_object: coupled excitatory and inhibitory population-firing variables on a large-scale structural connectome
hypotheticals.abstract: Resting-state cortical dynamics are commonly captured by coupled excitatory and inhibitory population-firing variables at each parcel, wired together through the empirical white-matter connectome. Earlier analyses emphasized regular ring or small-motif connectivity; here we characterize spontaneous spatial symmetry breaking on large, heterogeneous connectomes with realistic nodal-degree distributions. Linearizing the neural-mass equations about the spatially uniform low-firing equilibrium and expanding perturbations in the eigenbasis of the connectome Laplacian yields a discrete dispersion relation, the graph analogue of a continuous wavenumber spectrum. When the effective long-range spread of the inhibitory field across association fibers sufficiently exceeds that of the recurrent excitatory drive, the quiescent equilibrium, though stable to uniform fluctuations, is destabilized along specific Laplacian eigenmodes. Rather than periodic cortical stripes, the network partitions into persistently hyperactive and hypoactive regions; only parcels whose nodal connectivity falls near a critical value participate in the split, and we derive a closed-form expression for that critical connectivity. Saturating firing-rate nonlinearities generate several coexisting activity configurations and history-dependent switching between them under slowly ramped global drive. A mean-field reduction over connectivity classes reproduces the multistable repertoire, indicating a purely topological route by which connectome heterogeneity organizes large-scale functional differentiation without spatial periodicity, with implications for interpreting individual variability in resting-state activity amplitude across cortical areas.

hypotheticals.generic_object: coupled agglomeration and congestion indices across a network of trading regions
hypotheticals.abstract: Why do symmetric spatial economies spontaneously polarize into prosperous cores and lagging peripheries even without underlying locational advantages? We model each region with two interacting state variables: a locally self-reinforcing measure of firm and skilled-labor agglomeration, and a countervailing congestion-and-cost index, coupled across an inter-regional network whose links encode trade and commuting flows. The network's weighted Laplacian governs spatial redistribution, replacing the continuous-space transport operators of standard core-periphery theory. Linearizing about the symmetric equilibrium in which all regions are identical and expanding disturbances in the Laplacian eigenbasis gives a discrete growth-rate spectrum indexed by network modes. When cost pressures propagate across the trade network markedly faster than agglomeration benefits, the homogeneous configuration, robust to uniform shocks, loses stability along particular modes, and the system self-organizes into distinct high-density and low-density regional classes rather than a smoothly graded gradient. Strikingly, only regions of intermediate connectivity tip, and we obtain an analytic threshold for this participating connectivity. Nonlinearity produces multiple coexisting spatial equilibria and path dependence, so identical fundamentals can support divergent regional hierarchies depending on history. A mean-field approximation over connectivity strata predicts the emergent core share, offering a tractable account of endogenous polarization on realistic mobility networks and clarifying when integration policy amplifies rather than reduces disparities.

hypotheticals.generic_object: two coupled internal states of agents interacting over a large random communication graph
hypotheticals.abstract: Diffusive coupling is the standard mechanism for steering networked multi-agent systems toward consensus, yet coupling can also destabilize an agreement equilibrium and induce spontaneous clustering. We consider agents each carrying two internal states, one with fast local positive feedback and one providing slower negative feedback, exchanged with neighbors over a large Erdos-Renyi communication graph through the graph Laplacian. Linearizing the collective dynamics about the homogeneous consensus point and projecting perturbations onto the Laplacian eigenvectors yields a mode-indexed dispersion relation, the discrete analogue of a spatial-frequency stability curve. We show that when the coupling gain of the negative-feedback state exceeds that of the positive-feedback state beyond a computable ratio, consensus, though asymptotically stable under uniform disturbances, becomes unstable along a band of Laplacian modes. The agents then bifurcate into two self-consistent clusters of high and low state value; membership is governed almost entirely by each node's degree, and we give a closed-form critical degree predicting which agents defect from agreement. Saturating actuator nonlinearities admit several stable cluster assignments and hysteretic transitions under a slowly varying reference. A mean-field closure over degree classes accurately forecasts cluster sizes, providing design guidance for selecting coupling gains that either guarantee agreement or deliberately engineer clustering in distributed sensor, robotic, and power-electronic networks.

hypotheticals.generic_object: coupled state-of-charge and overpotential fields across a network of interconnected porous-electrode particles
hypotheticals.abstract: Composite intercalation electrodes comprise many active particles wired together by an electron-conducting matrix and a shared electrolyte, so that each particle's evolution depends on transport to and from its neighbors. We represent the electrode as a graph of particles carrying two coupled fields: a locally autocatalytic state of charge whose free energy favors phase separation, and a stabilizing local overpotential that redistributes charge across contacts. Neighbor exchange is encoded by the network Laplacian, standing in for continuum transport. Linear stability analysis about the uniformly charged state, with perturbations expanded in Laplacian eigenmodes, produces a discrete spectrum of growth rates. When the overpotential relaxes across the contact network sufficiently faster than the state of charge, the uniform filling, stable to collective fluctuations, is destabilized along select eigenmodes, and the ensemble splits into fully charged and nearly depleted particles instead of filling uniformly, a mosaic pathway reminiscent of many-particle bistability. Only particles whose contact number lies near a critical coordination participate, and we derive that critical coordination in closed form. The strongly nonlinear free energy supports numerous coexisting mosaic configurations and pronounced voltage hysteresis on charge-discharge cycling. A mean-field treatment grouped by coordination number reproduces the accessible states and the equilibrium voltage plateau, informing electrode microstructure design for uniform particle utilization, reduced local degradation, and improved rate capability.

hypotheticals.generic_object: coupled phosphorus-recycling and hypolimnetic-oxygen dynamics across a network of hydrologically connected lakes
hypotheticals.abstract: Chains and networks of lakes and reservoirs are hydrologically linked by channels and groundwater exchange, allowing dissolved constituents to move between basins. We ask whether an initially uniform, clear-water network can spontaneously split into eutrophic and clear basins without any external gradient in nutrient loading. Each basin is described by two coupled variables: a self-amplifying pool of recycled phosphorus sustained by sediment internal loading, and a suppressing hypolimnetic oxygen-and-mixing variable that damps that recycling; inter-basin exchange along the flow network is represented by the weighted graph Laplacian in place of a continuous dispersal term. A linear stability analysis about the uniform low-nutrient equilibrium, with disturbances decomposed onto Laplacian eigenmodes, produces a discrete dispersion relation. When the oxygenating variable is exchanged through the network appreciably faster than phosphorus, the homogeneous clear state, resilient to basin-wide perturbations, destabilizes along particular eigenmodes, and the network differentiates into persistently turbid and clear basins rather than a smooth trophic gradient. Only basins whose hydrologic connectivity lies near a critical value flip, following a simple threshold law we derive. The nonlinear internal-loading feedback yields alternative stable network configurations and marked hysteresis under gradually changing external inputs, implying that restoration targets are path dependent. A connectivity-class mean-field model predicts the fraction of basins that turn eutrophic.

queries.method_core: An analytic framework that encodes an ensemble's arbitrary discrete local-connectivity (offspring/incidence) distribution in probability generating functions and, under a tree-like local-independence (branching-process) assumption, exploits self-composition of those generating functions to locate the critical threshold at which a single extensive, self-sustaining cluster first emerges. Closed-form results for the emergence threshold, the probability and size of the extensive cluster, the expected number of elements reachable within a bounded radius, and distribution moments then follow from derivatives of the generating functions evaluated at unit argument.
queries.query_object: large real-world networks (e.g. hyperlink and scientific-collaboration structures) modeled as random graphs with a prescribed vertex-degree distribution

hypotheticals.generic_object: the onward-transmission structure of a host population with a heterogeneous, arbitrary secondary-case (offspring) distribution
hypotheticals.abstract: Classical outbreak models assume each infectious host generates a Poisson-distributed number of secondary cases, yet contact-tracing data repeatedly reveal strong individual-level heterogeneity, with a minority of hosts responsible for a disproportionate share of onward transmission. We develop a general framework for outbreak dynamics in populations with an arbitrary secondary-case (offspring) distribution. Encoding this distribution in a probability generating function, and treating early spread as a branching process in which transmission chains are locally tree-like, we obtain closed-form expressions for the invasion threshold separating self-limiting clusters from self-sustaining epidemics, the probability that a single introduction ignites a major outbreak, and the expected number of cases reachable within a given number of transmission generations. Iterating the generating-function recursion yields the full distribution of minor-outbreak sizes and, above threshold, the fraction of the population ultimately infected. We extend the treatment to asymmetric transmission between demographic strata and to settings where infectiousness and susceptibility are governed by separate distributions. Applied to empirical secondary-case counts from respiratory and vector-borne pathogens, the theory reproduces observed final-size and extinction statistics with striking fidelity in some settings, whereas systematic departures in others point to correlations in contact structure not captured by the assumption of independent transmission events.

hypotheticals.generic_object: branched macromolecules assembled from monomers whose number of reactive sites follows an arbitrary functionality distribution
hypotheticals.abstract: The onset of an infinite network during step-growth polymerization, the gel point, and the partition of material between soluble and network fractions govern the processability and ultimate properties of thermosets. We present a cascade theory for branched macromolecules assembled from monomers whose number of reactive sites follows an arbitrary functionality distribution, generalizing treatments restricted to a single monomer type. The functionality distribution is encoded in a probability generating function; assuming reaction proceeds without intramolecular cyclization so that growing molecules are treelike, self-consistent composition of the generating function yields the critical extent of reaction at which a macroscopic network first appears. Below this conversion we obtain the number- and weight-average molar masses and the full molecular-weight distribution from derivatives of the generating function evaluated at unit argument; above it we obtain the weight fraction incorporated into the infinite network (the gel fraction) and the diminishing sol. We further treat co-polymerizations in which distinct reactive groups react only with their complementary partners, and systems with unequal group reactivities. Comparison with titrimetric and rheological gel-point determinations for several multifunctional resin formulations shows quantitative agreement where cyclization is negligible, whereas gel points measured later than predicted quantify the extent of wasted intramolecular reaction in more dilute or flexible systems.

hypotheticals.generic_object: neutron cascades in a fissile assembly with an arbitrary neutron-multiplicity distribution per fission
hypotheticals.abstract: Predicting whether a fissile assembly sustains a divergent neutron chain, and characterizing the statistics of subcritical chains for source-driven and safeguards applications, requires accounting for the full distribution of neutrons emitted per fission rather than its mean alone. We formulate the neutron cascade as a branching process in which each induced fission releases a random number of prompt neutrons drawn from an arbitrary multiplicity distribution, encoded in a probability generating function. Under the assumption that successive neutron generations are statistically independent, valid when spatial and energy correlations are weak, self-composition of the generating function locates the critical condition separating chains that almost surely terminate from those with finite probability of unbounded growth, recovering unit multiplication factor as the threshold. We derive the probability that a single initiating neutron induces a divergent chain, the mean and variance of the neutron count in successive generations, and the expected total number of fissions in a terminating chain, all as derivatives of the generating function at unit argument. The formalism naturally accommodates delayed-neutron emission and multiple fissile species with distinct yield spectra. Predicted multiplicity moments and chain-length distributions agree closely with correlated-counting measurements on subcritical configurations, while residual discrepancies track spatial leakage effects omitted by the point-kinetics assumption.

hypotheticals.generic_object: sparse random constraint-satisfaction instances with an arbitrary variable-occurrence and clause-arity distribution
hypotheticals.abstract: Random instances of sparse constraint-satisfaction problems undergo abrupt solvability transitions as the ratio of constraints to variables increases, and the location of these thresholds controls the difficulty of the hardest instances for search procedures. Prior analyses largely assumed every variable participates in a Poisson-distributed number of constraints; real instance ensembles and structured encodings instead exhibit heavy-tailed, irregular participation. We give a generating-function analysis of ensembles in which each variable's number of constraint incidences, and each constraint's arity, follow prescribed arbitrary distributions. Representing these distributions by their probability generating functions and modeling the local incidence neighborhood as a tree, exact in the sparse limit, we derive the critical constraint density at which an extensive, mutually-dependent core of clauses first crystallizes, the point beyond which naive unit-propagation-style peeling fails. From derivatives of the generating functions at unit argument we obtain the expected number of variables within a bounded reasoning radius, the mean size of the residual core, and the fraction of variables it engulfs above threshold. The treatment extends to encodings with distinct positive- and negative-incidence spectra. On synthetic ensembles with engineered incidence distributions, predicted peeling thresholds and core sizes match empirical solver behavior closely, whereas deviations on structured instances expose incidence correlations that violate the tree approximation.

hypotheticals.generic_object: aftershock cascades in which each event triggers an arbitrary number of subsequent events (arbitrary triggering-productivity distribution)
hypotheticals.abstract: Whether a mainshock initiates a self-limiting aftershock sequence or a prolonged, cascading swarm depends on how many subsequent events each shock triggers, a quantity that varies strongly with magnitude and cannot be captured by its average alone. We treat triggered seismicity as a branching process in which every event spawns a random number of direct offspring events drawn from an arbitrary productivity distribution, encoded through a probability generating function. Assuming that triggering lineages are locally treelike, each aftershock attributable to a single parent, self-consistent composition of the generating function yields the critical branching ratio separating sequences that decay to background from those with finite probability of runaway proliferation. We obtain closed-form expressions for the probability that a triggering event founds a large sequence, the expected total number of events in a decaying sequence, and the mean count of descendants within a given number of triggering generations, each as derivatives of the generating function at unit argument. The framework accommodates depth- and magnitude-dependent productivity and multiple source regions with distinct triggering spectra. Fitted to regional catalogs, the theory reproduces observed sequence-size and duration statistics accurately in some tectonic settings, whereas systematic overdispersion elsewhere signals spatial clustering of triggering not represented by the independent-lineage assumption.

queries.method_core: Analysis of a two-state contagion/turnover process (each node alternates between an active 'infected' and an inactive 'susceptible' state, with activation transmitted along edges and spontaneous return to inactivity) running on a network whose degree distribution is heavy-tailed (power-law), using a degree-stratified heterogeneous mean-field approximation that writes coupled rate equations per connectivity class. The load-bearing result is that when the second moment of the degree distribution diverges, the critical transmission threshold separating global extinction from a persistent active steady state collapses to zero, so the process survives and reaches a finite endemic level for arbitrarily weak coupling.
queries.query_object: propagation of self-replicating computer viruses over the heavy-tailed connectivity of the Internet

hypotheticals.generic_object: a directly transmitted parasite dispersing across a fragmented host metapopulation
hypotheticals.abstract: Fragmented landscapes couple local host populations into metapopulations whose between-patch dispersal is markedly uneven: a few well-connected patches act as movement hubs while most exchange migrants with only a handful of neighbours. We ask how this heterogeneity in patch connectivity governs the fate of a directly transmitted parasite introduced into such a system. Extending patch-occupancy transmission theory, we stratify patches by their number of dispersal links and derive coupled rate equations for the fraction of infected patches in each connectivity class under a susceptible-infected-susceptible turnover of local outbreaks. When the distribution of dispersal degree is narrow, invasion requires per-contact transmissibility to exceed a positive critical value, recovering the familiar basic-reproduction-number threshold. However, when the degree distribution is broad and its variance grows without bound with system size, the invasion threshold collapses to zero: even parasites with vanishingly small transmissibility establish and settle at a finite endemic patch prevalence, sustained by rare super-connected patches that repeatedly re-seed the landscape. Numerical iteration of the stratified equations and stochastic patch simulations confirm the analytic prediction and reveal prevalence scaling anomalously with transmissibility near the origin. These results imply that highly heterogeneous connectivity can render fragmented host networks structurally incapable of excluding a parasite, with direct consequences for reserve design, corridor placement, and the management of wildlife and plant pathogens in patchy habitats.

hypotheticals.generic_object: distress propagation across an interbank exposure network
hypotheticals.abstract: Interbank obligations link financial institutions into an exposure network whose topology is strongly heterogeneous: a few money-centre institutions hold obligations to hundreds of counterparties, whereas the typical institution is tied to only a few. We study how this dispersion in counterparty degree shapes the propagation of distress. Treating an institution as either solvent or distressed, allowing distress to transmit along exposures and to be cured through recapitalisation, we write mean-field balance equations for the distressed fraction within each counterparty-degree class. For homogeneous networks the model reproduces the standard result that contagion dies out unless the per-exposure loss-given-default exceeds a strictly positive critical value. When the counterparty-degree distribution is fat-tailed with a diverging second moment, that critical value vanishes: arbitrarily small per-exposure loss rates suffice to sustain a self-perpetuating pocket of distress across the system, concentrated on and repeatedly re-ignited by the most connected hubs. We calibrate the stratified equations to synthetic exposure networks and confirm that the steady-state distressed fraction rises continuously from zero as leverage grows, with no protective threshold. The analysis reframes systemic risk as a structural property of the exposure network rather than of any individual balance sheet, and suggests that targeted capital surcharges on the most connected intermediaries are disproportionately effective at restoring a finite contagion threshold, whereas uniform requirements leave the heavy-tailed core exposed.

hypotheticals.generic_object: connectivity growth during random step-growth (branching) polymerization of multifunctional monomers
hypotheticals.abstract: Step-growth polymerisation of multifunctional monomers builds macromolecules whose connectivity can, at a critical extent of reaction, span the entire sample as an infinite network - the gel point. Classical branching theory locates this point at a conversion set by the mean and variance of the monomer functionality distribution. Here we treat mixtures in which functionality is extremely polydisperse: most monomers bear two reactive groups, but a small, heavy-tailed population carries very many, so that the second moment of the functionality distribution diverges as the ensemble grows. Writing generating-function recursions for the probability that a randomly chosen bond leads only to a finite branch, and stratifying monomers by functionality class, we show that the usual finite critical conversion collapses to zero. In such mixtures an incipient sample-spanning network forms at infinitesimal extent of reaction: no matter how few bonds have been made, a macroscopic connected component already exists, nucleated on and propagated through the rare high-functionality monomers. The weight-average degree of polymerisation diverges from the outset, and the sol fraction departs from unity with an anomalous power law in conversion. We verify these predictions by kinetic Monte Carlo on random monomer ensembles. The result implies that trace amounts of highly branched cross-linkers can abolish the gelation threshold entirely - a design principle for instantaneously percolating gels and a caution for the storage stability of reactive formulations.

hypotheticals.generic_object: cascading component outages across a high-voltage transmission grid
hypotheticals.abstract: Cascading outages are the dominant mechanism behind large-area blackouts: the failure of one element redistributes flow and can trip its neighbours, which trip theirs, and so on. The topology of high-voltage transmission grids is uneven, with a minority of substation buses aggregating a large number of incident lines. We investigate how this dispersion in bus connectivity controls whether a local disturbance dies out or propagates system-wide. Representing each element as either in-service or tripped, with tripping transmitted to connected elements above a per-line stress and restoration returning elements to service, we derive mean-field balance equations for the tripped fraction in each connectivity class. On grids with narrow connectivity the model yields a positive critical stress below which disturbances self-extinguish, consistent with N-1 security intuition. When the distribution of incident-line counts is broad enough that its second moment grows without bound, this critical stress vanishes: arbitrarily mild per-line stress sustains a persistent, self-reigniting outage fraction anchored on the most heavily interconnected buses. Iterating the stratified equations and running quasi-steady cascade simulations on synthetic grids confirms that the steady-state outage fraction grows continuously from zero with loading, eliminating any protective margin. We conclude that connectivity heterogeneity, not average redundancy, sets blackout vulnerability, and that hardening or islanding the few highest-degree buses restores a finite threshold far more efficiently than uniform reinforcement of the network.

hypotheticals.generic_object: a novel linguistic variant diffusing across a speaker interaction network
hypotheticals.abstract: Linguistic innovations - a new pronunciation, a reanalysed construction, a borrowed discourse marker - spread from speaker to speaker through the fabric of everyday interaction. That fabric is highly unequal: a few socially central speakers converse with very many interlocutors, while most have a modest circle. We model how this inequality in interaction connectivity conditions whether an incipient variant catches on or fades. Each speaker is treated as currently favouring the innovative or the conservative variant, with the innovative form transmitted through interaction and speakers occasionally reverting, and we write mean-field equations for the innovating fraction within each connectivity class. When interaction is homogeneous, the variant propagates community-wide only if its per-interaction transmissibility - its social or communicative advantage - exceeds a positive critical value; below it the innovation dies out, offering a mechanism for failed changes. When the distribution of interlocutor counts is heavy-tailed and its variance diverges with community size, that critical advantage falls to zero: even a variant with negligible intrinsic advantage stabilises at a finite frequency across the community, kept alive by the most connected speakers who continually re-seed it. Iterating the equations reproduces the characteristic rise from zero without a threshold and predicts anomalous frequency scaling near the actuation point. The account suggests that the connectivity structure of a speech community, not merely the prestige of a form, can determine which innovations actuate and diffuse.

queries.method_core: Exact derivation of the order-disorder threshold for a system of coupled two-state (binary) units interacting over a heterogeneous random network with an arbitrary connectivity (degree) distribution P(k), obtained by closing the dynamics with a self-consistent mean-field equation for a collective order parameter. The threshold is set by the moments of P(k) — chiefly the ratio of the mean degree to the second moment — so that heavy-tailed connectivity, for which low-order moments diverge, yields anomalous critical behavior: the threshold runs to a limiting value, the transition becomes of infinite order, and finite-size corrections are anomalously strong.
queries.query_object: nearest-neighbor ferromagnetic Ising spins on an equilibrium random graph with an arbitrary degree distribution P(k)

hypotheticals.generic_object: a directly transmitted contagion spreading over a host contact network with an arbitrary distribution of contacts per individual
hypotheticals.abstract: We derive the exact invasion threshold for a contagion propagating over an equilibrium contact network whose number of contacts per individual follows an arbitrary distribution P(k). Modeling each host as occupying one of two states, susceptible or infectious, with recovery returning it to susceptibility, we close the transmission dynamics through a self-consistent equation for the stationary prevalence and obtain the threshold in closed form. The critical per-contact transmissibility separating a contagion that dies out from a self-sustaining endemic state is fixed entirely by the moments of the contact distribution, scaling as the ratio of the mean number of contacts to its second moment. For contact patterns with a fat tail — populations harboring rare, extremely well-connected superspreaders — we find anomalous behavior in the endemic prevalence, in the sensitivity of prevalence to transmissibility, and in the variance of final outbreak size, precisely when the second moment of P(k) diverges in the large-population limit. In that regime the invasion threshold collapses toward zero, so a persistent outbreak can be seeded at arbitrarily low transmissibility; the onset of endemicity becomes infinitely smooth rather than sharp, and finite-population corrections are unusually large, decaying slowly with the number of hosts. We discuss consequences for surveillance sensitivity and for targeted immunization of highly heterogeneous host populations, where removing a handful of the most connected individuals restores a finite threshold.

hypotheticals.generic_object: binary adoption states of individuals embedded in a social-influence network with an arbitrary distribution of ties
hypotheticals.abstract: We obtain the exact tipping point for the collective adoption of a behavior diffusing through a social-influence network in which the number of ties per individual follows an arbitrary distribution P(k). Each actor is represented as a binary unit — adopter or non-adopter — whose propensity to adopt rises with the share of adopting neighbors, and we resolve the resulting peer-influence dynamics through a self-consistent equation for the aggregate adoption level. The critical strength of interpersonal influence at which a population shifts from near-universal non-adoption to widespread adoption is determined by the tie distribution, depending on the ratio between the average number of ties and the mean squared number of ties. When ties are heavy-tailed — a population dominated by a few extremely well-connected influentials — the aggregate adoption level, its responsiveness to marginal changes in influence, and the dispersion of eventual reach all display anomalous behavior once the second moment of the tie distribution diverges. In this regime the tipping point recedes toward zero, so even weak social pressure can ignite society-wide diffusion; the passage from minority to majority adoption ceases to be abrupt and instead unfolds with an anomalously gradual onset, and the outcome stays strongly sensitive to the size of the community. We draw out implications for seeding strategies in viral marketing and collective-action campaigns, where influence concentrated on the most connected members disproportionately governs whether a cascade takes off.

hypotheticals.generic_object: binary solvent/default states of institutions coupled through an interbank exposure network with an arbitrary distribution of counterparties
hypotheticals.abstract: We compute in closed form the critical level of financial stress at which an interbank system tips from stability into a system-wide default cascade, for a counterparty network whose number of exposures per institution follows an arbitrary distribution P(k). Each institution is treated as a binary unit, either solvent or in default, its condition responding to the states of its counterparties through shared exposures, and the contagion is resolved via a self-consistent equation for the fraction of the system in default. The resilience threshold separating a shock absorbed locally from one that propagates to engulf the whole network is governed by the moments of the exposure distribution, scaling with the ratio of the mean number of counterparties to its second moment. When the exposure network is heavy-tailed — a market organized around a few enormously interconnected hub institutions — the equilibrium default fraction, its sensitivity to a marginal deterioration in conditions, and the spread of cascade sizes all behave anomalously as the second moment of the connectivity distribution diverges. In that limit the resilience threshold falls toward zero, leaving the system fragile to vanishingly small shocks; the passage from stability to collapse loses its sharp character and becomes anomalously smooth, and finite-system corrections remain large even for markets with many participants. We draw consequences for macroprudential capital surcharges levied on systemically central institutions.

hypotheticals.generic_object: binary operational/failed states of substations in a transmission network with an arbitrary distribution of interconnections
hypotheticals.abstract: We derive the exact loading threshold beyond which localized component outages escalate into a grid-spanning blackout, for a transmission network whose number of interconnections per substation follows an arbitrary distribution P(k). Each node is modeled as a binary element, operational or tripped, whose status depends on the operational status of the buses to which it is coupled through shared lines, and the cascade is resolved by a self-consistent balance equation for the fraction of the network that has failed. The robustness threshold demarcating a disturbance that is contained from one that propagates system-wide is fixed by the moments of the interconnection distribution, varying as the ratio of the mean connectivity to its second moment. For networks with a heavy-tailed connectivity profile — topologies dominated by a small number of very highly connected hub substations — the steady-state failed fraction, its marginal sensitivity to increased loading, and the variability of cascade extent all exhibit anomalous behavior once the second moment of P(k) diverges in the large-network limit. In this regime the robustness threshold drops toward zero, so arbitrarily light additional loading can trigger network-wide collapse; the onset of large-scale failure becomes anomalously gradual rather than a sharp transition, and finite-network effects persist strongly even as the number of substations grows. We assess implications for hardening priorities and redundancy allocation in heterogeneous infrastructure networks.

hypotheticals.generic_object: binary active/quiescent states of neurons on a structural connectivity network with an arbitrary distribution of connections
hypotheticals.abstract: We obtain the exact critical synaptic coupling at which a recurrent neuronal network switches from a quiescent baseline to self-sustained collective activity, for a connectivity architecture in which the number of connections per neuron follows an arbitrary distribution P(k). Each neuron is idealized as a binary unit, active or silent, whose probability of activation grows with the activity of the cells that project to it, and the population dynamics are closed through a self-consistent equation for the mean activation level. The critical coupling separating a network that relaxes to silence from one that ignites into persistent firing is determined by the moments of the connectivity distribution, scaling as the ratio of the average number of connections per neuron to its second moment. When the connectome is heavy-tailed — an architecture containing a minority of massively connected hub neurons — the stationary activation level, its sensitivity to a marginal change in coupling, and the fluctuations of population activity all behave anomalously as the second moment of P(k) diverges in the large-network limit. In this regime the ignition threshold recedes toward zero, so self-sustained activity can be maintained at arbitrarily weak coupling; the transition into persistent firing loses its sharpness and becomes anomalously smooth, and finite-size corrections stay unusually strong even for large populations. We consider consequences for the excitability, dynamic range, and avalanche statistics of empirically heavy-tailed cortical networks.

queries.method_core: A framework for constructing and analyzing directed interaction networks of coupled units by three complementary routes: (i) rule-based/stochastic generative models that place directed links according to simple probabilistic attachment rules; (ii) coupled nonlinear dynamical systems for the units' state variables whose equilibria are tested for local stability by linearizing and inspecting the spectrum of the Jacobian; and (iii) sequential-growth models in which the network self-assembles through iterative addition of new units followed by a selection filter that retains only viable additions, yielding large persistent structures.
queries.query_object: directed networks of trophic (predator-prey) interactions among biological species

hypotheticals.generic_object: directed inter-firm input-output / supply networks
hypotheticals.abstract: We survey theoretical treatments of production networks, in which firms or sectors are joined by directed supplier-customer relationships. Following a summary of empirical input-output tables and firm-level transaction records, we organize the literature into three modeling traditions. The first comprises static generative models, which place directed sourcing links between agents according to simple stochastic rules calibrated to observed degree and trade-weight distributions. The second comprises dynamical models, in which the output levels of coupled producers evolve under nonlinear adjustment equations; here we concentrate on the resilience of the economy's equilibrium, characterizing when a fixed point of aggregate activity is locally stable by linearizing the coupled system and examining the spectrum of its Jacobian. Larger, more densely interlinked economies are generically harder to stabilize, reflecting a complexity-fragility tension. The third tradition builds the network endogenously: firms enter through founding or through imitation of incumbents' input recipes, and a selection filter removes entrants that cannot recover costs, so the architecture self-organizes rather than being imposed exogenously. We show that entry-and-selection processes can assemble large, persistent production structures with realistic layering and supply-cycle statistics, and that the resulting equilibria are markedly more robust than those of comparably sized randomly wired economies. We close by relating cascade-of-failure vulnerability to the generative rules that shaped the underlying topology.

hypotheticals.generic_object: directed gene regulatory networks
hypotheticals.abstract: Theoretical models of gene regulatory circuitry are reviewed. After summarizing genome-wide expression compendia and inferred transcription-factor interaction maps, we distinguish three classes of model. The first class are static ensemble models, which wire activating and repressing regulatory edges among genes by simple probabilistic rules and study the resulting connectivity statistics. The second class are dynamical models, in which coupled ordinary differential equations govern the abundances of transcripts and proteins across interacting loci; we emphasize the robustness of steady expression states, deriving conditions under which a fixed point of the regulatory system is asymptotically stable from the eigenvalues of the linearized dynamics. We highlight that richly interconnected circuits are not automatically stable and that specific motif structure is required to reconcile high connectivity with dynamical robustness. The third class are growth models, in which the network is enlarged over evolutionary time by duplication of existing loci followed by divergence of their regulatory inputs, with a selection step retaining only variants that preserve viable expression programs. We find that such duplication-and-divergence assembly can construct large, homeostatically stable regulatory architectures displaying the modularity and degree heterogeneity seen in real organisms, whereas equivalent randomly rewired circuits are typically dynamically fragile. We conclude by discussing how mutational network growth shapes the evolvability of developmental control.

hypotheticals.generic_object: directed software package dependency networks
hypotheticals.abstract: We review models of software dependency ecosystems, where packages are nodes joined by directed 'requires' relationships. Beginning with an overview of empirical dependency graphs mined from public package registries, we group modeling efforts into three families. The first family consists of static generators, which attach dependency edges between components using simple probabilistic attachment rules tuned to reproduce observed in- and out-degree distributions and reuse frequencies. The second family consists of dynamical models, in which the load, version churn, or fault state of mutually dependent components co-evolves under coupled update equations; we focus on stability, asking when a healthy operating point of the ecosystem resists propagating breakage, and answer it by linearizing the coupled dynamics and inspecting the leading eigenvalue of the interaction matrix. We note that more densely coupled ecosystems tend toward fragility, so that heavy reuse trades functionality against susceptibility to cascading regressions. The third family consists of growth models, in which the ecosystem is built incrementally: new components are published either de novo or by forking and adapting existing ones, and an adoption filter prunes packages that attract no downstream users. We demonstrate that such publish-and-select growth assembles large, long-lived dependency structures with the layering, heavy-tailed reuse, and near-decomposability observed in real repositories, and that these are more resilient to maintainer dropout than comparably sized random graphs.

hypotheticals.generic_object: directed power transmission / flow networks
hypotheticals.abstract: Modeling approaches for electric power transmission networks are surveyed, treating buses as nodes and directed real-power flows along transmission corridors as links. After reviewing measured grid topologies and flow records, we organize methods into three categories. The first category is static topology generators, which lay down transmission links between substations by simple stochastic construction rules reproducing empirical line-length, degree, and loading distributions. The second category is dynamical, describing the coupled electromechanical swing behavior of interconnected generators through nonlinear phase-angle equations; we concentrate on stability, establishing when a synchronized operating state is small-signal stable by linearizing about the equilibrium and evaluating the eigenvalues of the reduced system, and we show how added interconnection can either damp or, counterintuitively, undermine synchrony. The third category comprises expansion-planning and self-organized-growth models, in which the network is enlarged incrementally by commissioning new lines and generation, with a reliability-and-cost filter rejecting additions that fail security screening. We find that incremental build-out under such selection can produce large grids that remain transiently and voltage-stable at scale, exhibiting the sparse, hierarchically clustered structure of real interconnections, whereas randomly augmented grids of the same size are markedly more prone to cascading outages. We relate blackout-size statistics to the construction rules that generated the underlying topology.

hypotheticals.generic_object: directed chemical reaction networks among molecular compounds
hypotheticals.abstract: Theoretical models of chemical reaction networks are reviewed, with compounds as nodes and directed reaction transformations as links. Following a survey of curated reaction databases and mass-spectrometric inventories of complex mixtures, we distinguish three model classes. The first class are static combinatorial models, which connect reactants and products by simple rule-based schemes, such as bond-formation grammars, and characterize the resulting network topology. The second class are dynamical models, in which coupled nonlinear rate equations govern the concentrations of interacting compounds; we focus on the stability of stationary states, deriving conditions under which a steady concentration profile is asymptotically stable from the Jacobian of the mass-action kinetics, and noting that more highly connected reaction systems are not generically stable. The third class are constructive models, in which the network is grown from a small seed set by iteratively introducing new compounds via catalyzed transformations of those already present, retaining only additions that remain producible and non-depleting under the ambient flux. We show that such closure-driven growth can assemble large, self-maintaining autocatalytic organizations that persist far from equilibrium, reproducing the sparse, hierarchical connectivity of complex reaction mixtures, whereas randomly assembled networks of comparable size typically collapse to trivial steady states. We discuss implications for the emergence of self-sustaining chemistry from simple precursor pools.
